answer:
Definition

Express Middleware performance bottlenecks appear when middleware execution order, synchronous operations, or redundant processing in the middleware chain becomes the throughput ceiling. At advanced level, this includes: middleware ordering causing unnecessary work, memory leaks in stateful middleware, blocking I/O in auth middleware, and lack of short-circuiting for static/health routes.

Concrete Use-Case

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj's Express.js API had 8 middleware layers executing on every request — including JSON body parsing, CORS, JWT verification, rate limiting, request logging, and permission checks. For the GET /health endpoint (called every 5 seconds by the load balancer), all 8 middleware layers ran unnecessarily, adding 15ms overhead × 12 health checks/minute = wasted CPU at scale.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Before: All middleware runs on every request
app.use(cors());
app.use(express.json({ limit: '10mb' }));
app.use(rateLimiter);
app.use(jwtAuth);           // 8ms sync JWT verify
app.use(permissionCheck);
app.use(requestLogger);

// Health check buried after all middleware
app.get('/health', (req, res) => res.json({ status: 'ok' }));

// After: Short-circuit for health/static routes
// 1. Health check BEFORE any middleware
app.get('/health', (req, res) => res.json({ status: 'ok' }));
app.get('/metrics', (req, res) => res.send(metricsRegistry.metrics()));

// 2. Lightweight middleware for all routes
app.use(cors());
app.use(requestLogger);

// 3. Conditional body parsing (skip for GET requests)
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  if (req.method === 'GET' || req.method === 'HEAD') return next();
  express.json({ limit: '10mb' })(req, res, next);
});

// 4. Auth middleware (async, with caching)
app.use(async (req, res, next) => {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  if (!token) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Missing token' });

  // Cache verified tokens for 5 minutes
  const cached = tokenCache.get(token);
  if (cached) { req.user = cached; return next(); }

  try {
    const payload = await jwtVerify(token, JWKS);
    tokenCache.set(token, payload, 300000);
    req.user = payload;
    next();
  } catch {
    res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid token' });
  }
});

// 5. Rate limiting after auth (so we can rate limit per user)
app.use(rateLimiter);

// 6. Permission check only on mutation routes
app.use('/api', (req, res, next) => {
  if (req.method === 'GET') return next();
  return permissionCheck(req, res, next);
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: CRM API response times increased by 200ms after adding a new audit logging middleware that wrote every request to MongoDB. Under load, the API became unresponsive during quarterly reporting.

Investigation: Manoj profiled the middleware chain with timing hooks. The audit middleware was synchronously awaiting MongoDB write for every request (including reads). Under 500 concurrent users, the MongoDB connection pool for audit writes was exhausted, blocking the main request pipeline.

Fix: (1) Made audit logging fire-and-forget (no await). (2) Buffered audit logs in memory, flushing to MongoDB every 5 seconds in batch. (3) Only audit write operations (POST/PUT/DELETE), not reads. (4) Added a circuit breaker — if MongoDB audit writes fail 3 times, stop auditing for 60 seconds. Result: audit overhead from 200ms to 0.1ms (non-blocking).

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
All middleware runs on every route including health checksRegister health/metrics routes before middleware stack
Body parsing on GET requests wastes CPUConditional parsing: only parse body for POST/PUT/PATCH
Sync JWT verification blocks event loopAsync JWT with JWKS caching; cache verified tokens in LRU
Audit/logging middleware blocks request pipelineFire-and-forget with batched writes; circuit breaker on failure

Validation Strategy

  • Middleware timing: add onRequest and onResponse hooks to measure per-middleware overhead
  • Health check: verify /health responds in < 5ms with zero middleware overhead
  • Load test: 500 concurrent requests, verify middleware chain adds < 10ms total overhead
  • Memory test: run for 24 hours, verify no memory growth from middleware state (token cache bounded)

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Express Middleware examines the middleware pattern's trade-offs (composability vs. hidden dependencies), failure modes (error middleware ordering, async error swallowing, memory leaks in closures), advanced patterns (route-specific middleware stacks, middleware factories), and rollback strategies when middleware changes break the request pipeline.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a layered middleware architecture for the Fastify API (using Express-compatible patterns): (1) Security layer (helmet, CORS, rate limiting), (2) Auth layer (JWT verification, role extraction), (3) Context layer (correlation ID, request timing), (4) Validation layer (Zod schema validation per route). The key trade-off: every middleware adds latency and complexity, but removing middleware creates security gaps.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Middleware factory pattern with feature flags
function createMiddlewareStack(config: MiddlewareConfig) {
  const stack: RequestHandler[] = [];

  // Security layer (always enabled)
  stack.push(helmet());
  stack.push(cors({ origin: config.corsOrigins }));

  // Rate limiting (feature-flagged)
  if (config.rateLimiting.enabled) {
    stack.push(createRateLimiter(config.rateLimiting));
  }

  // Auth layer
  stack.push(asyncHandler(async (req, res, next) => {
    const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
    if (!token) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
    req.user = await verifyToken(token);
    next();
  }));

  // Context enrichment
  stack.push((req, res, next) => {
    req.correlationId = req.headers['x-correlation-id'] || crypto.randomUUID();
    req.startTime = performance.now();
    res.setHeader('x-correlation-id', req.correlationId);
    next();
  });

  return stack;
}

// Async error wrapper (critical for Express 4)
function asyncHandler(fn: (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => Promise) {
  return (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => {
    fn(req, res, next).catch(next); // Forward async errors to error middleware
  };
}

// Global error middleware (MUST be registered last)
app.use((err: Error, req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => {
  const correlationId = req.correlationId || 'unknown';
  console.error({
    correlationId,
    error: err.message,
    stack: err.stack,
    path: req.path,
    method: req.method,
    userId: req.user?.id,
  });

  if (err.name === 'ValidationError') {
    return res.status(400).json({ error: err.message, correlationId });
  }
  if (err.name === 'UnauthorizedError') {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Authentication failed', correlationId });
  }
  res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error', correlationId });
});

// Route-specific middleware (not all routes need all middleware)
const publicRoutes = express.Router();
publicRoutes.get('/health', (req, res) => res.json({ status: 'ok' }));
publicRoutes.get('/api/public/categories', getCategories);

const protectedRoutes = express.Router();
protectedRoutes.use(...createMiddlewareStack(config));
protectedRoutes.get('/api/leaves', getLeaves);
protectedRoutes.post('/api/leaves', validateLeave, createLeave);

app.use(publicRoutes);
app.use(protectedRoutes);

Debugging Narrative

Situation: The HR API started returning 500 errors intermittently. The error logs showed "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'id')" but no stack trace pointing to the actual middleware that failed.

Investigation: Manoj traced the issue to the permission check middleware, which accessed req.user.id — but on some requests, the JWT middleware had silently failed (async rejection not caught) and req.user was undefined. Express 4 didn't propagate the async error, so the request continued to the next middleware with no user attached.

Fix: (1) Wrapped all async middleware with the asyncHandler utility that catches promise rejections and calls next(err). (2) Added a guard in permission middleware: if req.user is undefined, return 401 immediately. (3) Added middleware ordering validation on startup: verify error handler is registered last, auth middleware before permission middleware. (4) Migrated critical paths to Fastify which handles async errors natively.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Async errors silently swallowed in Express 4asyncHandler wrapper that calls next(err)
Error handler not registered lastStartup validation that checks middleware order
Hidden state mutations via req propertiesTypeScript augmentation: declare req.user type; guard checks
Middleware changes break pipeline silentlyIntegration tests that verify full middleware chain per route

Validation Strategy

  • Error propagation test: throw error in each middleware, verify it reaches the error handler with correlation ID
  • Ordering test: startup check verifies error handler is the last middleware registered
  • Async test: reject a promise in auth middleware, verify 401 returned (not process crash)
  • Feature flag test: disable rate limiting via config, verify requests still flow through correctly

Definition

Fastify Plugins performance bottlenecks at scale involve plugin encapsulation overhead, decorator namespace collisions, hook execution cost in deeply nested plugin trees, and connection pool management across plugins. Fastify's encapsulated plugin system is powerful but can introduce hidden performance costs when plugins are poorly structured.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj structured the Fastify app with nested plugins: auth-plugin (JWT verification), leave-plugin (routes + business logic), notification-plugin (Socket.io), and database-plugin (Prisma client). The bottleneck: each plugin registered its own onRequest hook, and with 5 nested plugin levels, every request triggered 12 hook executions — many redundant.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Before: Hooks registered at root level — run on ALL routes
app.addHook('onRequest', authHook);        // Runs even on /health
app.addHook('onRequest', loggingHook);     // 12 hooks per request
app.addHook('onRequest', rateLimitHook);

app.register(leavePlugin, { prefix: '/api/leaves' });
app.register(notifyPlugin, { prefix: '/api/notifications' });
app.get('/health', async () => ({ status: 'ok' }));

// After: Encapsulated hooks — run only where needed
// Health check — no hooks
app.get('/health', async () => ({ status: 'ok' }));

// API routes with encapsulated middleware
app.register(async function apiRoutes(api) {
  // These hooks only run for routes inside this plugin
  api.addHook('onRequest', authHook);
  api.addHook('onRequest', rateLimitHook);

  api.register(leavePlugin, { prefix: '/leaves' });
  api.register(notifyPlugin, { prefix: '/notifications' });
}, { prefix: '/api' });

// Leave plugin with its own encapsulated hooks
async function leavePlugin(fastify: FastifyInstance) {
  // This hook only runs for /api/leaves/* routes
  fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (req) => {
    // Validate leave-specific permissions
    if (!req.user.permissions.includes('leave:read')) {
      throw fastify.httpErrors.forbidden('Missing leave permissions');
    }
  });

  fastify.get('/', async (req) => {
    return fastify.prisma.leave.findMany({ where: { employeeId: req.user.id } });
  });

  fastify.post('/', {
    schema: {
      body: leaveCreateSchema, // JSON Schema for validation
    },
  }, async (req) => {
    return fastify.prisma.leave.create({ data: req.body });
  });
}

// Decorator pattern for shared services (registered once)
app.register(async function dbPlugin(fastify) {
  const prisma = new PrismaClient({
    datasources: { db: { url: process.env.DATABASE_URL } },
  });
  await prisma.$connect();
  fastify.decorate('prisma', prisma);
  fastify.addHook('onClose', () => prisma.$disconnect());
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After adding a request validation plugin to the HR system, all API response times increased by 5ms — even routes that didn't use validation.

Investigation: Manoj used Fastify's built-in request timing (reply.elapsedTime) and found that the validation plugin registered an onRequest hook at the root level. This hook checked for a schema property on every route — even routes without schemas. With 50+ routes, this added 5ms of unnecessary schema lookup per request.

Fix: (1) Moved the validation hook inside the API plugin scope (encapsulated). (2) Used Fastify's built-in schema validation (via route schema option) instead of a custom hook — Fastify compiles schemas at startup with fast-json-stringify, making validation near-zero cost at runtime. (3) Added ajv compilation caching for custom schemas. Result: validation overhead from 5ms to 0.2ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Root-level hooks run on all routes including health checksRegister hooks in encapsulated plugin scopes
Decorator namespace collisions between pluginsUse fastify.hasDecorator() check before decorating
Plugin initialization order causes undefined decoratorsUse fastify-plugin for shared decorators; after() for ordering
Deeply nested plugins make debugging difficultLimit nesting to 3 levels; use Fastify's printPlugins() for visualization

Validation Strategy

  • Hook count: log total hooks per request path; verify no redundant executions
  • Health check isolation: verify /health triggers zero application hooks
  • Schema compilation: verify schemas compiled at startup, not at request time
  • Plugin tree: run app.printPlugins(), verify tree depth ≤ 3

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Fastify Plugins covers the encapsulated plugin model, decorator pattern for dependency injection, lifecycle hooks (onRequest, preHandler, preSerialization, onSend), schema-based validation and serialization, failure modes (circular dependencies, plugin timeout), and rollback strategies for plugin updates.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj architected the Fastify application as a plugin tree: a database plugin (Prisma + Redis clients as decorators), an auth plugin (JWT verification hooks), a websocket plugin (Socket.io integration), and domain plugins (leave, approval, calendar). The challenge: ensuring the database plugin loads before domain plugins that depend on fastify.prisma, while keeping plugins encapsulated and independently testable.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Database plugin — shared across all plugins via fastify-plugin
import fp from 'fastify-plugin';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
import { createClient } from 'redis';

export default fp(async function databasePlugin(fastify, opts) {
  const prisma = new PrismaClient({
    log: [{ emit: 'event', level: 'query' }],
  });

  // Query performance monitoring
  prisma.$on('query', (e) => {
    if (e.duration > 100) {
      fastify.log.warn({ query: e.query, duration: e.duration }, 'Slow query detected');
    }
  });

  await prisma.$connect();
  fastify.decorate('prisma', prisma);

  const redis = createClient({ url: opts.redisUrl });
  await redis.connect();
  fastify.decorate('redis', redis);

  // Cleanup on shutdown
  fastify.addHook('onClose', async () => {
    await prisma.$disconnect();
    await redis.disconnect();
  });
}, {
  name: 'database-plugin',
  dependencies: [], // No dependencies
});

// Auth plugin — depends on database plugin
export default fp(async function authPlugin(fastify) {
  fastify.decorateRequest('user', null);

  fastify.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
    const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
    if (!token) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized();

    // Check token blacklist in Redis
    const blacklisted = await fastify.redis.sIsMember('token-blacklist', token);
    if (blacklisted) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('Token revoked');

    try {
      req.user = await verifyJWT(token);
    } catch {
      throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('Invalid token');
    }
  });
}, {
  name: 'auth-plugin',
  dependencies: ['database-plugin'], // Ensures DB loads first
});

// Leave domain plugin — encapsulated (own scope)
async function leavePlugin(fastify: FastifyInstance) {
  // Permission hook only runs for routes in this plugin
  fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (req) => {
    if (!req.user.permissions.includes('leave:manage')) {
      throw fastify.httpErrors.forbidden();
    }
  });

  // Route with JSON Schema validation + serialization
  fastify.post<{ Body: CreateLeaveInput }>('/', {
    schema: {
      body: {
        type: 'object',
        required: ['startDate', 'endDate', 'type'],
        properties: {
          startDate: { type: 'string', format: 'date' },
          endDate: { type: 'string', format: 'date' },
          type: { type: 'string', enum: ['ANNUAL', 'SICK', 'PERSONAL'] },
        },
      },
      response: {
        201: { $ref: 'LeaveResponse' },
      },
    },
  }, async (req, reply) => {
    const leave = await fastify.prisma.leave.create({ data: req.body });
    reply.code(201).send(leave);
  });
}

// App composition with guaranteed load order
const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
app.register(databasePlugin, { redisUrl: process.env.REDIS_URL });
app.register(authPlugin);
app.register(leavePlugin, { prefix: '/api/leaves' });

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After refactoring the HR system into Fastify plugins, the leave approval endpoint started throwing "fastify.prisma is not a function" errors on every other deployment.

Investigation: Manoj found a race condition in plugin registration. The leave plugin was registered with app.register() without ensuring the database plugin had finished loading. Fastify loads plugins in parallel by default unless explicitly ordered. On some deployments, the leave plugin's ready hook fired before the database plugin decorated fastify.prisma.

Fix: (1) Added dependencies: ['database-plugin'] to all plugins that use fastify.prisma. (2) Used fastify-plugin (fp) wrapper for shared plugins, which breaks encapsulation and makes decorators available to sibling plugins. (3) Added a startup validation hook that checks all required decorators exist before accepting traffic. (4) Added integration tests that verify the full plugin tree loads correctly.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Plugins load in parallel — race conditionsUse dependencies array in fastify-plugin options
Encapsulated plugin can't access parent decoratorsUse fastify-plugin wrapper to share decorators across scopes
Plugin error during load crashes the entire appAdd error handler in app.ready(); graceful startup failure
Testing plugins in isolation requires mocking decoratorsUse fastify.inject() with test-specific plugin composition

Validation Strategy

  • Load order test: add a startup hook that logs plugin load order; verify database → auth → domain
  • Decorator test: before accepting traffic, check fastify.hasDecorator('prisma')
  • Isolation test: mount each plugin independently with mocked dependencies; verify routes work
  • Graceful shutdown: send SIGTERM, verify all onClose hooks run (Prisma disconnect, Redis close)

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into JWT lifecycle covers the complete token lifecycle: issuance (claims, expiry, signing algorithm), verification (signature, expiry, audience, issuer), refresh flow (rotation, revocation), storage (httpOnly cookies vs. localStorage), failure modes (token theft, replay attacks, clock skew), and rollback strategies for key rotation.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented JWT auth with: (1) Access token (15 min expiry, RS256 signed, contains userId, role, departmentId), (2) Refresh token (7 day expiry, stored in httpOnly cookie, hashed in PostgreSQL), (3) Token rotation on each refresh (old refresh token invalidated), (4) Token blacklisting on logout via Redis set with TTL matching access token expiry.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { SignJWT, jwtVerify, createRemoteJWKSet } from 'jose';
import { createPrivateKey, createPublicKey } from 'crypto';

// Key pair for RS256 (stored securely, rotated quarterly)
const privateKey = createPrivateKey(process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY);
const publicKey = createPublicKey(process.env.JWT_PUBLIC_KEY);

// Issue access token
async function issueAccessToken(user: { id: string; role: string; departmentId: string }) {
  return new SignJWT({
    sub: user.id,
    role: user.role,
    departmentId: user.departmentId,
  })
    .setProtectedHeader({ alg: 'RS256', kid: 'key-2025-Q1' })
    .setIssuedAt()
    .setExpirationTime('15m')
    .setIssuer('hr-leave-api')
    .setAudience('hr-leave-client')
    .sign(privateKey);
}

// Issue refresh token (opaque, stored hashed in DB)
async function issueRefreshToken(userId: string) {
  const token = crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('hex');
  const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(token).digest('hex');
  const familyId = crypto.randomUUID(); // Token family for reuse detection

  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      tokenHash,
      userId,
      familyId,
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000),
    },
  });

  return token;
}

// Refresh flow with rotation and reuse detection
app.post('/api/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (!refreshToken) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('No refresh token');

  const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
  const stored = await prisma.refreshToken.findUnique({ where: { tokenHash } });

  if (!stored || stored.expiresAt < new Date()) {
    // Possible token reuse — invalidate entire family
    if (stored) {
      await prisma.refreshToken.deleteMany({ where: { familyId: stored.familyId } });
      fastify.log.warn({ userId: stored.userId, familyId: stored.familyId }, 'Refresh token reuse detected — family invalidated');
    }
    throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('Invalid refresh token');
  }

  if (stored.used) {
    // Token was already used — replay attack! Invalidate entire family
    await prisma.refreshToken.deleteMany({ where: { familyId: stored.familyId } });
    throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('Token reuse detected');
  }

  // Mark current token as used
  await prisma.refreshToken.update({ where: { tokenHash }, data: { used: true } });

  // Issue new tokens
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: stored.userId } });
  const accessToken = await issueAccessToken(user);
  const newRefreshToken = await issueRefreshToken(user.id);

  // Update family ID for rotation chain
  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true,
    secure: true,
    sameSite: 'strict',
    path: '/api/auth',
    maxAge: 7 * 24 * 60 * 60,
  });

  return { accessToken };
});

// Logout — blacklist access token + revoke refresh token
app.post('/api/auth/logout', async (req, reply) => {
  const accessToken = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  if (accessToken) {
    const { payload } = await jwtVerify(accessToken, publicKey);
    const ttl = Math.max(0, payload.exp - Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000));
    await redis.setEx(`blacklist:${accessToken}`, ttl, '1');
  }

  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (refreshToken) {
    const hash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
    await prisma.refreshToken.delete({ where: { tokenHash: hash } });
  }

  reply.clearCookie('refreshToken');
  return { message: 'Logged out' };
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: An HR admin reported that after logging out, they could still access the API for several minutes using the same access token. Additionally, a user claimed someone else accessed their account without their credentials.

Investigation: Manoj found two issues: (1) The logout endpoint deleted the refresh token from the database but didn't blacklist the access token — it remained valid until its 15-minute expiry. (2) The refresh token was stored in localStorage (not httpOnly cookie), making it vulnerable to XSS. A malicious browser extension had stolen the refresh token and generated new access tokens.

Fix: (1) Added access token blacklisting on logout using Redis Set with TTL = remaining token expiry. (2) Moved refresh token from localStorage to httpOnly secure cookie with SameSite=Strict. (3) Added refresh token rotation — each refresh generates a new token and invalidates the old one. (4) Implemented token family tracking — if an old refresh token is reused, the entire family is invalidated (detecting stolen tokens).

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Access token valid after logoutBlacklist in Redis with TTL matching remaining expiry
Refresh token stolen via XSShttpOnly secure cookie with SameSite=Strict; never localStorage
Refresh token reuse after theftToken rotation + family tracking; invalidate family on reuse
Key rotation breaks existing tokensJWKS with key overlap: serve old + new keys for 24h

Validation Strategy

  • Logout test: after logout, verify access token returns 401 immediately
  • Rotation test: use refresh token, verify old token no longer works
  • Reuse detection: use an old (rotated) refresh token, verify entire family invalidated
  • Key rotation: issue token with old key, verify still valid during overlap window

Definition

Architecting JWT lifecycle for 1M+ users requires a token system that handles massive issuance/verification throughput, efficient blacklisting at scale, key rotation without service disruption, and cross-service token validation in a microservices architecture.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled HR Leave Management System serving 1M employees, Manoj would architect: asymmetric JWT (RS256) so any service can verify tokens without the private key, a distributed JWKS endpoint cached by all services, Redis-based token blacklisting (bloom filter for memory efficiency), and per-department token scoping to limit blast radius of compromised tokens.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Bloom filter-based token blacklisting at scale
import { BloomFilter } from 'bloom-filters';

// Bloom filter: ~1MB for 1M tokens with 0.1% false positive rate
const tokenBlacklist = BloomFilter.create(1_000_000, 0.001);

// On logout or token revocation
async function revokeToken(token: string, expiresAt: number) {
  const tokenId = extractJTI(token); // JWT ID claim
  tokenBlacklist.add(tokenId);

  // Also store in Redis for persistence across restarts
  const ttl = Math.max(0, expiresAt - Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000));
  await redis.setEx(`revoked:${tokenId}`, ttl, '1');
}

// Verification middleware (called 167K times/second)
async function verifyToken(req, reply) {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  if (!token) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized();

  // Step 1: Verify signature (cached JWKS)
  const { payload } = await jwtVerify(token, JWKS, {
    issuer: 'hr-auth-service',
    audience: 'hr-api',
  });

  // Step 2: Check blacklist (bloom filter — O(1), <0.01ms)
  if (tokenBlacklist.has(payload.jti)) {
    // Bloom filter can have false positives — confirm with Redis
    const revoked = await redis.get(`revoked:${payload.jti}`);
    if (revoked) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('Token revoked');
  }

  // Step 3: Scope validation
  if (payload.scope && !payload.scope.includes(req.routeOptions.url)) {
    throw fastify.httpErrors.forbidden('Token scope insufficient');
  }

  req.user = payload;
}

// JWKS endpoint with key rotation support
app.get('/.well-known/jwks.json', async () => {
  return {
    keys: [
      // Current key
      { kid: 'key-2025-Q1', ...currentPublicKeyJWK, use: 'sig', alg: 'RS256' },
      // Previous key (kept during rotation window)
      ...(rotationInProgress
        ? [{ kid: 'key-2024-Q4', ...previousPublicKeyJWK, use: 'sig', alg: 'RS256' }]
        : []),
    ],
  };
});

// Scoped tokens for department isolation
async function issueScoped Token(user, scope: string[]) {
  return new SignJWT({
    sub: user.id,
    role: user.role,
    departmentId: user.departmentId,
    scope, // e.g., ['/api/leaves/*', '/api/calendar/*']
    jti: crypto.randomUUID(), // Unique ID for blacklisting
  })
    .setProtectedHeader({ alg: 'RS256', kid: 'key-2025-Q1' })
    .setExpirationTime('15m')
    .setIssuer('hr-auth-service')
    .setAudience('hr-api')
    .sign(privateKey);
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After a security audit at scale (500K active users), the team discovered that token blacklisting was consuming 2GB of Redis memory — far more than expected. Additionally, blacklist lookups were taking 5ms due to Redis network round-trip on every request.

Investigation: Manoj found that the blacklist stored full JWT strings (1.5KB each) instead of just the JTI claim (36 bytes). With 500K revoked tokens, this consumed 750MB in Redis. The additional memory came from fragmentation. Also, every request made a Redis network call to check the blacklist.

Fix: (1) Stored only JTI in Redis (36 bytes vs. 1.5KB — 97% reduction). (2) Added an in-process Bloom filter as first-layer check — if the token isn't in the Bloom filter, skip Redis entirely (98% of tokens). (3) Set TTL on blacklist entries matching token expiry (auto-cleanup). (4) On process startup, rebuilt Bloom filter from Redis. Result: Redis memory from 2GB to 50MB, blacklist check from 5ms to 0.01ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Blacklist memory grows unboundedTTL matching token expiry; store only JTI (not full token)
Redis roundtrip per request for blacklist checkIn-process Bloom filter as fast first-layer check
Key rotation breaks in-flight tokensJWKS serves both old and new keys during 24h rotation window
Compromised token has wide access scopeScoped tokens with path-based permissions; department isolation

Validation Strategy

  • Blacklist performance: verify < 0.1ms per check with Bloom filter + Redis fallback
  • Memory audit: track Redis blacklist memory, verify proportional to active revocations
  • Key rotation: rotate keys, verify all services pick up new JWKS within 10 minutes
  • Scope test: issue scoped token, verify access denied outside scope boundaries
